reasoning: The Southern Ocean's circumpolar current — the Antarctic Circumpolar Current (ACC) — is the strongest ocean current on Earth, driven by persistent westerly winds (the Roaring Forties and Furious Fifties) that arise from Coriolis deflection of poleward-flowing air. The >15% wind asymmetry is a well-documented feature of Southern Hemisphere meteorology: the absence of large continental landmasses allows unimpeded zonal flow. NOAA/ECMWF reanalysis data sets (ERA5, NCEP) have documented this for decades. The dome labels this a "Southern Ocean aetheric current" but provides no mechanism distinct from standard Coriolis-driven circulation. Registration date (2026-03-28) postdates published data by many decades.
